coredata.dc:title: Comparative study of synthesis of carbon quantum dots via different routes: Evaluating doping agents for enhanced photoluminescence emission
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown Copyright © 2024 Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Mirlou-Miavagh, Faraz
Rezvani-Moghaddam, Amir
Roghani-Mamaqani, Hossein
Sundararaj, Uttandaraman
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent inks based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) find versatile applications in both security and biotechnology sectors. They serve as a potent tool for anti-counterfeiting applications such as military documents, banknotes, valuable certificates, and checks. In this study, we embarked on the synthesis of CQDs through a one-step procedure employing citric acid, ethylene diamine, and aniline, in varying ratios, utilizing three distinct synthesis methods including hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and simple heating techniques. Comprehensive characterization techniques, such as UV–Visible spectroscopy, Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM), and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), were employed to investigate the physicochemical properties of the synthesized carbon quantum dots. Among the various CQD samples synthesized in this study, the best one was selected as a functional dye for the formulation of fluorescent ink. The ink was subsequently applied to paper using inkjet printing techniques. To delve further into the interaction between the ink and paper substrate, water contact angle measurements were performed on both printed areas and unprinted paper. Notably, the results revealed a decrease in contact angle after printing, indicating enhanced ink penetration into the porous structure of the paper.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Anti-counterfeiting
Inkjet
Quantum yield
Photoluminescence
Citric acid
Contact angle
Microwave-assisted processing
Aniline
Hydrothermal method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Nitrogen Dopes (N-CQDs) and un-doped (CQD) Carbon Quantum Dots
Optical characterization of carbon quantum dots
Morphological characterization of synthesized quantum dots

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: Crown Copyright © 2024 Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of different N-CQD and CQDs' synthesis methods, a) Hydrothermal method, b) Microwave-assisted method, and c) Simple heating method.
UV–Visible spectra of a) CQD, b) N-A-CQD, and c) N-E-CQD samples using hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and simple heating methods, respectively.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of synthesized CQDs via a) hydrothermal, b) microwave-assisted, and c) simple heating methods excited at 278 nm. PL spectra of N-A-CQD samples synthesized via d) hydrothermal excited in the range of 290-330 nm, e) microwave-assisted, and f) simple heating methods excited in the range of 310 to 350 nm. PL spectra of N-E-CQD samples synthesized via g) hydrothermal excited in the range of 310 to 350 nm, h) microwave-assisted, and i) simple heating methods excited in the range of 320 to 360 nm.
a) FE-SEM image of N-E-CQD doped via ethylene diamine that shows 7.5 nm and b) FE-SEM image of N-A-CQD doped samples via aniline with the size of 8.5 nm.
TEM analysis and size distribution of N-E-CQD synthesized via microwave-assisted method.
Evaluating the printed paper under daylight and UV irradiation (20 W UVA Lamp), in 1,5,10 and 15 times of repetition of printing using the formulated ink with N-E-CQD.
Water Contact Angle analysis from the printed surface of the paper and the un-printed surface of the paper. As shown, the printed surface has a lower contact angle than the un-printed surface.
EDS analysis of N-E-CQD and N-A-CQD samples.

head.ce:title: Comparative study of synthesis of carbon quantum dots via different routes: Evaluating doping agents for enhanced photoluminescence emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Polymer Engineering, Sahand University of Technology, P.O. Box: 51335–1996, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Polymer Engineering, Sahand University of Technology, P.O. Box: 51335–1996, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Polymeric Materials, Sahand University of Technology, P.O. Box: 51335–1996, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering, University of Calgary, Calgary, AB, T2N 1N4, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Polymer Engineering, Sahand University of Technology, P.O. Box: 51335–1996, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent inks based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) find versatile applications in both security and biotechnology sectors. They serve as a potent tool for anti-counterfeiting applications such as military documents, banknotes, valuable certificates, and checks. In this study, we embarked on the synthesis of CQDs through a one-step procedure employing citric acid, ethylene diamine, and aniline, in varying ratios, utilizing three distinct synthesis methods including hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and simple heating techniques. Comprehensive characterization techniques, such as UV–Visible spectroscopy, Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM), and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), were employed to investigate the physicochemical properties of the synthesized carbon quantum dots. Among the various CQD samples synthesized in this study, the best one was selected as a functional dye for the formulation of fluorescent ink. The ink was subsequently applied to paper using inkjet printing techniques. To delve further into the interaction between the ink and paper substrate, water contact angle measurements were performed on both printed areas and unprinted paper. Notably, the results revealed a decrease in contact angle after printing, indicating enhanced ink penetration into the porous structure of the paper.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescence refers to the emission of light from a substance arising from electronic excitation processes. It can be categorized into two primary forms: fluorescence and phosphorescence, each determined by the specific nature of electronic excitation. Fluorescence emission is associated with the excitation of electrons to higher energy states within orbitals that have paired spin with electrons in the ground state. This excitation results in relatively short emission lifetimes, typically lasting around 10 ns. On the other hand, phosphorescence involves the emission of light from triplet excited states, where electrons in these states share the same spin orientation as electrons in the ground state. This configuration has an inhibited transition to the ground state, resulting in slower emission rates. Consequently, phosphorescent materials exhibit longer lifetimes, typically ranging from milliseconds to seconds [1].

A fluorophore is a molecule characterized by its unique fluorescence properties. These molecules have the capacity to absorb photons and subsequently emit them at a lower energy level. Typically, fluorophores are composed of multiple bonded aromatic groups or consist of planar or cyclic structures with numerous π bonds. This distinctive property makes them versatile in various applications. Fluorophores can be employed as tracers within liquids or to selectively stain specific structures in addition to substrates for enzymes and probes for indicators. Their fluorescence response is often influenced by environmental factors such as polarity and the presence of ions [2,3]. More commonly, they are covalently attached to larger macromolecules, serving as markers (dyes, tags, reporters) for affinity-based or bioactive reagents (e.g., antibodies, peptides, nucleic acids). Fluorophores serve as valuable tools for a variety of applications, including staining tissues, cells, or materials, particularly in the field of analytical techniques such as fluorescence imaging and spectroscopy. Their ability to emit detectable light under specific conditions allows researchers to gain valuable insights into biological and chemical processes, making them invaluable in scientific exploration and diagnostics. On the other hand, the issue of fraudulent copying and counterfeiting is a serious problem that transcends societal boundaries. It has the potential to inflict significant damage on various aspects of society, particularly the economy, through the production of counterfeit goods, money, and forged documents. Furthermore, counterfeit goods pose a substantial threat to public health, especially in the case of counterfeit medications, which can have profound mental and physical repercussions for individuals. To mitigate these adverse effects, researchers have exhibited a great interest in developing novel, cost-effective methods with enhanced security measures to reduce counterfeiting.

Quantum dots have gained significant attention as promising nanomaterials for fluorescent applications. Quantum dot-based fluorescent nanomaterials can be classified into three categories, each defined by its distinct physical and chemical properties: inorganic quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [4]. Notably, over the past few decades, Cadmium Selenide (CdSe) and Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) quantum dots have garnered considerable interest due to their exceptional light-emitting characteristics [5]. However, it is important to acknowledge that these inorganic quantum dots pose a risk to human health and can potentially lead to carcinogenic effects, limiting their applications primarily to sensing purposes. Consequently, researchers have been diligently exploring environmentally friendly alternatives to mitigate pollution and create non-toxic materials, addressing these concerns.

Carbon quantum dots can be synthesized through two distinct methods: the top-down approach and the bottom-up approach. The top-down approach involves breaking large carbon particles into smaller ones using techniques such as discharge, electrochemical oxidation, sonication, and chemical oxidation. However, these methods typically need expensive equipment and lengthy reaction times [6]. Conversely, the bottom-up approach involves the transformation of individual carbon atoms into carbon quantum dots of the desired size. This method can be achieved through various techniques, including hydrothermal treatment, thermal decomposition, pyrolysis, carbonization, microwave-assisted synthesis, and solvothermal methods. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) typically exhibit sizes within the range of 1–10 nm [5,7]. Moreover, they can be doped with a variety of different atoms, such as nitrogen, boron, phosphorus, and sulfur [8–14].

Zhang and colleagues [15] conducted a study involving the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots, aimed at assessing their suitability for spraying when applied to paper. In another study, Sain and co-workers [16] synthesized nitrogen-doped CQDs, employing trans-Aconitic acid as a carbon precursor and diethylenetriamine as a doping agent. These synthesized CQDs exhibited a notably high quantum yield of 81 %, making them suitable for applications in switch sensing and imaging. Carinhana et al. [17] focused on the synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped carbon quantum dots using phytic acid and L-arginine as precursors. The resulting N-CQD and P-CQD were utilized for the detection of Cr (VI) ions in water and soil samples. In a different study, Dong and co-workers [18] investigated the synthesis of fluorine-doped carbon quantum dots using 4,5-difluorobenzene-1,2-diamine. Their work yielded yellow-emitting F-CQD, which found applications in red cell imaging and sensitive intracellular Ag + ion detection.

Joseph et al. [19] synthesized phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots using trisodium citrate as a precursor and phosphoric acid as a phosphorus doping agent. The resulting material served as an effective fluorometric probe for iron detection. The presence of an amorphous region surrounding the core of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) plays a pivotal role in influencing their optical properties, with this effect being directly linked to the reaction temperature. Elevated reaction temperatures tend to result in the complete carbonization of the amorphous region. This, in turn, can lead to an increase in the size of the carbon core, causing a noticeable shift in the photoluminescence emission towards longer wavelengths. Innocenzi et al. [20] observed that as the temperature rises, the carbon core grows, and the amorphous region over the core diminishes. Conversely, at lower temperatures, the formation of a carbonaceous core becomes challenging. The optimal condition for achieving the highest quantum yield lies in an intermediate temperature range where both the core and the shell components coexist.

As highlighted, the properties of synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be greatly influenced by the choice of doping agents, the reaction temperature, and the specific synthesis routes employed. Recognizing the multifaceted impact of these parameters, our study systematically explored their effects by employing three distinct synthesis routes characterized by varying reaction temperatures and doping agents. This approach helped us better understand how these important factors affect CQD properties. Consequently, the synthesized CQDs demonstrated remarkable attributes, including a pronounced fluorescent emission, and outstanding printability properties. These findings underscore the significance of tailoring the synthesis process to engineer desirable characteristics into CQDs for a wide range of applications.

Aniline and 1,2-ethylene diamine were purchased from Merck Co. (Germany). Just before use, aniline was purified using a two-component distillation method. Citric acid was acquired from Dr. Mojallali Co. (Iran) and was used directly without any additional purification. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone was sourced from Sigma-Aldrich Co., while both glycerin and Polyethylene glycol (PEG) were obtained from Dr. Mojallali Co.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Nitrogen Dopes (N-CQDs) and un-doped (CQD) Carbon Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs were synthesized through three distinct methods: hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and simple heating, utilizing two different doping agents, 1,2-ethylene diamine, and aniline. For the synthesis of N-CQDs with 1,2-ethylene diamine as the doping agent (N-E-CQD), a mixture containing 1 g of citric acid, 0.3 ml of ethylene diamine, and 50 ml of double-distilled water was thoroughly mixed. In the case of the synthesis of N-CQDs with aniline as the doping agent (N-A-CQD), the synthesis mixture involved 1 g of citric acid, 0.3 ml of distilled aniline, and 50 ml of double-distilled water. As for undoped CQDs, they were synthesized by introducing 1 g of citric acid and 50 ml of double-distilled water into the reaction mixture. It is worth noting that the concentrations of reactants were kept constant for synthesizing N-E-CQD, N-A-CQD, and CQD across the various synthesis routes.

The hydrothermal synthesis method is a highly effective process that takes place under elevated temperatures and pressures, resulting in the creation of carbon quantum dots with uniform sizes and exceptional quantum yields. This technique is versatile and can be seamlessly applied to both small-scale and large-scale production, making it a valuable tool for a wide range of applications. In the hydrothermal method, the synthesis process began by dissolving citric acid in water at 60 °C, allowing the mixture to be stirred for 30 min. After this initial step, the solution was cooled down to room temperature, and a nitrogen doping agent was introduced.

The next stage involved transferring the reaction mixture into a Teflon-lined autoclave, where it was maintained at 200 °C for a duration of 12 h. After reaction, the produced carbon quantum dots were separated and purified. This was achieved by passing the mixture through a 220 nm Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) syringe filter. Subsequently, the filtered quantum dots were subjected to a freeze-drying process and stored in a desiccator under a nitrogen atmosphere for future analysis. Fig. 1 -a offers a schematic illustration of the entire process. This method allows for the controlled and efficient production of carbon quantum dots with tailored properties, making it an essential technique in various scientific and industrial applications.

One of the key advantages of the microwave-assisted method, in comparison to other conventional synthesis techniques, is its significantly reduced synthesis time. This approach relies on the efficient utilization of microwave irradiation to rapidly heat precursors. The homogeneous distribution of energy throughout the reaction mixture simplifies the overall synthesis process, enabling the easy production of fluorescent carbon quantum dots. In just a few minutes, these quantum dots can be synthesized with outstanding quantum yields and at a relatively low cost. This remarkable efficiency and cost-effectiveness make the microwave-assisted method a compelling choice for the rapid and economical production of high-quality carbon quantum dots. In the microwave-assisted synthesis method, as seen in Fig. 1-b, a microwave oven was utilized. In the initial step, 1 g of citric acid was dissolved in 50 ml of double-distilled water at 60 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature, and the nitrogen doping agent was added. Next, all the components were transferred into a beaker, and a microwave oven operating at 800 watts was employed to complete the reaction in 10 min. In the following step, an additional 20 ml of double-distilled water was introduced into the beaker. The resulting mixture was dispersed for an additional 2 h. After this, the synthesized carbon quantum dots were subjected to filtration using a 220 nm PTFE syringe filter. The filtered quantum dots were then dried through freeze-drying. The resulting materials were kept under a nitrogen atmosphere in a desiccator, for further investigations.

In the simple heating synthesis method, a reflux system was employed, as outlined in Fig. 1-c. The procedure was as follows: initially, 1 g of citric acid was dissolved in 50 ml of double-distilled water at 60 °C. Following this, the solution was carefully cooled down to room temperature, and the nitrogen doping agent was introduced. Depending on the specific doping requirements, either aniline or EDA (Ethylene Diamine) were employed. For the subsequent step, the reflux system was set and a continuous nitrogen purge was maintained throughout the reaction. This controlled environment was sustained for a duration of 20 h to facilitate the synthesis process effectively. After the prescribed reaction time, the apparatus was disassembled, and the resulting material was subjected to purification by passing it through a 220 nm PTFE filter. The filtered quantum dots were subsequently freeze-dried to eliminate any remaining solvents. The resulting samples were carefully stored within a desiccator, under a nitrogen atmosphere for further investigations.

The optimum nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots were chosen as the ideal pigment for formulating inkjet printing ink. To formulate the ink, 1 % by weight of quantum dots were dispersed in a mixture consisting of solvents, co-solvents, and additives (e.g., Glycerol, N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone, Deionized water, and surfactants) as the ink base provided form Borna Shimi Arya compony (Iran). The ink was then carefully loaded into the Epson T50 printer cartridge. The printing was done at varying repetition levels: 1, 5, 10, and 15 times. This approach allowed for the assessment of print quality and performance under different conditions, offering valuable insights into the capabilities of quantum dots for inkjet printing applications.

Ultraviolet-Visible absorption spectra were acquired using an Onlab-EU-2200R spectrometer (ONLAB Co., China) within the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were recorded with the FP-750 fluorometer (Jasco, Japan). For particle size measurements of CQD particles, the Dynamic Light Scattering method (DLS) was employed, utilizing the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, France). Scanning Electron Microscopy images were captured with a Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) Mira3 (Tescan, Czech Republic). Water contact angle and surface tension were assessed using the CAG-10 instrument (Jikan, Iran), while a T50 printer (Epson, Japan) was employed for the printing of fluorescent inks on paper. The Quantum yield
φ s
was calculated according to Eq. (1) [21], which involves various parameters. Specifically,
φ r
represents the quantum yield of standard Rhodamine 6G as the reference, while ms and mr represent the slope of the linear fit for the integrated fluorescence intensity as a function of the concentration of CQDs and the reference Rhodamine 6G, respectively. Additionally,
η r
and
η s
correspond to the refractive indices of the standard solution and the studied fluorescent material, respectively [22]. Detailed information regarding the calculation of the quantum yield of synthesized quantum dots is provided in the supplementary information.
(1)
φ s
=
φ r
m s
m r
η s
η r
2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 presents the UV–Vis spectra of both undoped and nitrogen-doped CQDs synthesized through three distinct methods. In Fig. 2-a, undoped CQDs produced using all three synthesis routes displayed a low-intensity absorbance peak, with a λmax measurement of 278 nm. Moving on to Fig. 2-b, the hydrothermal-synthesized N-A-CQD exhibited the highest intensity absorbance peak among the three materials. In contrast, the other two materials synthesized through microwave-assisted and simple heating methods exhibited lower absorbance levels, with their maximum absorbance peaks located at 320 nm. Fig. 2-c depicts the N-E-CQD sample, which demonstrated a high absorbance intensity across all three synthesis methods.

Notably, the microwave-assisted synthesized sample displayed even higher absorbance intensity compared to the other methods. The N-E-CQD samples exhibited an absorbance peak at a slightly longer wavelength of 340 nm. The differences in absorbance intensities observed in the UV–Vis spectra of the various carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized using different methods and doped with nitrogen can be attributed to several factors.

The size and morphology of CQDs can vary depending on the synthesis method. Smaller CQDs often have a higher surface area, which can lead to greater absorbance due to a higher number of available absorption sites. Additionally, nitrogen doping can alter the electronic structure of CQDs. It introduces energy levels within the bandgap, which can affect the absorption spectrum. The specific nitrogen-doping concentration and configuration in each sample may have played a role in the observed differences in absorbance intensity. Structural defects and surface states can also contribute to variations in absorbance intensity. The presence of defects can create additional energy levels in the bandgap, affecting the absorption spectrum. The differences in absorbance intensities among the CQDs synthesized through various methods and doped with nitrogen are likely the result of a combination of the mentioned factors which reveals the influence of the synthesis process on the optical properties of carbon quantum dots.

In this regard, the absorption peak observed in the range of 320 to 340 nm is likely attributable to the n-π* transition associated with conjugated CN or CO functional groups within the carbon quantum dots. This specific transition results from the excitation of electrons from non-bonding orbitals (n) to π* antibonding orbitals, and it is common in the UV–Vis spectra of molecules with conjugated systems.

Photoluminescence (PL) analysis was conducted to assess the emission spectra of the synthesized samples. The excitation range was carefully selected to be within ±20 nm of the maximum absorbance, as determined from the UV–Vis spectroscopy results. The PL emission spectra of diluted solutions of both undoped and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were examined in DI water at a concentration of 0.3 mg ml−1. In Fig. 3 -a, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum of undoped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized via the hydrothermal method revealed a pronounced peak at 550 nm. This vibrant emission was achieved by exciting the CQDs with light at a wavelength of 278 nm. Likewise, Fig. 3-b showed undoped CQDs synthesized through the microwave-assisted method, which exhibited a peak emission at 550 nm when excited at 278 nm, mirroring the emission pattern seen in the hydrothermal-synthesized CQDs. Fig. 3-c depicted undoped CQDs synthesized through a simple heating process, and, similar to the other methods, these CQDs displayed their maximum emission intensity at 550 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 278 nm. These consistent emission patterns across various synthesis methods suggest that the resulting undoped CQDs share a common photoluminescent behavior, with a prominent emission peak at 550 nm when excited at 278 nm.

Aniline nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-A-CQD) synthesized via the hydrothermal method displayed a distinct emission peak at 406 nm when excited within the range of 290 to 330 nm (Fig. 3-d). On the other hand, N-A-CQDs synthesized using the microwave-assisted route showed a lower fluorescence emission intensity. The excitation wavelength for this sample fell within the range of 310 to 350 nm, and the maximum emission intensity was observed at 450 nm (Fig. 3-e). Simple heating produced quantum dots also exhibited a lower fluorescence emission intensity. The excitation wavelength for this sample ranged from 310 to 350 nm, and the maximum emission intensity was 455 nm (Fig. 3-f).

As illustrated in Fig. 3-g, 3-h, and 3-i, diluted solutions of N-E-CQDs obtained through the hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and simple heating methods exhibited their maximum emission intensity at 470 nm. The excitation wavelength for all N-E-CQDs was set within the range of 310 to 360 nm. Notably, the microwave-assisted synthesis method yielded a higher emission intensity compared to the other two routes.

We observed that various synthesis methods and doping conditions yielded significant differences. The notable increase in both absorption and photoluminescence (PL) intensity in certain samples indicated an advanced carbonization process, resulting in the formation of larger and more highly conjugated carbon quantum dots (CQDs). These expanded CQDs may exhibit an elevated degree of graphitization, thereby enhancing their optical characteristics, for both absorbance and PL. The variations in emission characteristics among these materials suggest that the choice of synthesis method and doping conditions can be tailored to achieve specific desired optical properties, allowing for enhanced versatility in their design and usage.

Based on the reported data, the hydrothermal-synthesized CQDs and N-A-CQDs, as well as the microwave-assisted-synthesized N-E-CQD samples, were identified as the optimal samples for further investigations. The quantum yield of the optimum carbon quantum dots was calculated by using Eq. (1) which was ∼98 % for N-E-CQD, ∼20 % for N-A-CQD, and ∼14 % for CQD, which showed a high quantum yield obtained for nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots via microwave assisted synthesis route. A higher quantum yield means that a greater proportion of the absorbed photons results in fluorescence emission, which is reflected in the increased PL intensity. The variations in emission characteristics and quantum yields of these materials suggest that the choice of synthesis method and doping conditions can be tailored to achieve specific desired optical properties, allowing for enhanced versatility in their design and usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization of synthesized quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological and size characteristics of optimal N-CQDs and CQD were comprehensively assessed through different techniques like Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM), and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analyses. These methods collectively provided insights into the size and morphology of the synthesized quantum dots. DLS analysis revealed that the size distribution of the synthesized quantum dots fell within the range of 6 nm to 10 nm. Specifically, DLS determined size distributions of 6 nm, 9.5 nm, and 7 nm for undoped CQD, N-A-CQD, and N-E-CQD, respectively. These results shed light on the uniformity and size consistency within the various quantum dot samples. In addition to DLS, FE-SEM was employed to study the morphology and size of the N-CQDs. For this purpose, a dilute dispersion of N-CQDs was deposited onto a laboratory glass slide and allowed to desiccate for 24 h at room temperature. Spherical nanoparticles with size distributions of 7.5 nm and 8.5 nm for each carbon quantum dot were observed. Figs. 4 -a and 4-b depict the FE-SEM analysis for N-E-CQD and N-A-CQD, respectively. For N-E-CQD, the analysis revealed particle sizes within a range of 7.5 ± 2 nm, while for N-A-CQD, the sizes were determined to be in the range of 8.5 ± 2 nm. These results provided a visual representation of the spherical morphology and size homogeneity of the carbon quantum dots under investigation.

The morphology and size distribution of the optimized N-E-CQD were also examined through Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the size distribution of the N-E-CQD sample was found to be in accordance with the results obtained from Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements. This consistency across multiple analytical methods underscores the precision and reliability of the findings.

Nitrogen atoms are introduced as heteroatoms within the carbon lattice during the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots. These nitrogen atoms can disrupt the regular carbon structure and may lead to the formation of larger, more complex carbon structures. The introduction of nitrogen atoms can create defects, alter the carbon lattice, and potentially lead to the incorporation of larger functional groups, resulting in larger carbon dots as illustrated in the DLS analysis. On the other hand, the size of quantum dots in the context of nitrogen doping can be influenced by the choice of the nitrogen source or dopant. In this case, the use of ethylene diamine (EDA) as a nitrogen-doping agent led to smaller quantum dot sizes when compared to the use of aniline as a nitrogen-doping agent. This size difference can be explained by reaction kinetics: The choice of nitrogen source can impact the reaction kinetics during the quantum dot synthesis process. Ethylene diamine, being a smaller molecule, may react more rapidly with the carbon precursor, leading to the formation of smaller quantum dots. Aniline, on the other hand, is a larger molecule and may react more slowly, resulting in larger quantum dots. Additionally, the molecular structure of the nitrogen-doping agent also plays a crucial role. Ethylene diamine has a linear structure with two amine groups, which can readily coordinate with the carbon precursor, leading to the formation of smaller quantum dots. Aniline, with its bulkier structure, might experience steric hindrance, reducing the efficiency of nitrogen doping and favoring the formation of larger quantum dots.
To further validate the successful formation of both N-A-CQD and N-E-CQD, Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDAX) was performed, and the results of this comprehensive analysis are detailed below.

Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDS) was employed to conduct a comprehensive chemical characterization of the synthesized N-CQDs. In this procedure, the dispersion of N-E-CQD and N-A-CQD samples were deposited onto the surface of a laboratory glass slide and subsequently allowed to air-dry for a period of 24 h at room temperature. The results of this analysis revealed a successful integration of the nitrogen atoms within the quantum dots. An in-depth elemental analysis of the elemental composition is presented in Table 1 . For the N-E-CQD sample, it was found that the quantum dots consisted of approximately 67 % by weight of Carbon, 24 % by weight of Oxygen, and 8 % by weight of Nitrogen atoms. These results clearly demonstrate the effective incorporation of Nitrogen within the quantum dot structure, indicating the successful synthesis of N-E-CQDs. On the other hand, the N-A-CQD sample exhibited a different elemental composition, with approximately 30 % by weight of Carbon, 68 % by weight of Oxygen, and 2 % by weight of Nitrogen atoms. This disparity in composition between N-E-CQD and N-A-CQD underscores the significance of the selection of the doping agents.

The difference in nitrogen doping levels when using aniline as a doping agent compared to ethylene diamine can be attributed to the distinct chemical properties and reactivity of these compounds. Aniline (C6H5NH2) contains a single amino group (−NH2) as its primary functional group. Ethylene diamine (H2NCH2CH2NH2), on the other hand, contains two amino groups. Each amino group in ethylene diamine can contribute a nitrogen atom to the quantum dots. As a result, ethylene diamine has a higher nitrogen content per molecule compared to aniline. On the other hand, ethylene diamine is a more potent reducing agent and has a higher reactivity with precursors used in quantum dot synthesis. This increased reactivity allows it to more efficiently incorporate nitrogen atoms into the quantum dot structure. Furthermore, ethylene diamine exhibits a notably superior solubility in water when contrasted with aniline. Ethylene diamine demonstrates a remarkable affinity for water, rendering it highly water-soluble. This compound readily dissolves in aqueous environments, resulting in a crystal-clear and colorless solution. The exceptional solubility of ethylene diamine in water can be attributed to its inherent ability for hydrogen bonding with water molecules, endowing it with polar characteristics. Conversely, aniline is only sparingly soluble in water. It is classified as a weak base and, as such, have limited solubility in aqueous solutions. The low solubility of aniline in water can be attributed to the nonpolar nature of its aromatic ring. This higher solubility may lead to better dispersion of the ethylene diamine in the reaction medium, promoting a more uniform and efficient incorporation of nitrogen atoms.

The fluorescence mechanism of N-doped carbon quantum dots encompasses a multifaceted interplay of structural, electronic, and surface properties. Nitrogen dopants introduce heteroatoms into the carbon lattice, thus creating localized energy levels within the bandgap and facilitating efficient radiative recombination processes. N-doped carbon quantum dots exhibit quantum confinement effects, effectively minimizing non-radiative recombination pathways and thereby enhancing photoluminescence efficiency. Furthermore, surface functionalization with nitrogen-containing groups and surface passivation play pivotal roles in reducing surface defects. Concurrently, charge transfer processes between the carbon matrix and surrounding molecules exert influence on excited-state dynamics.

Considering the above-mentioned discussions, N-E-CQD was the selected sample for an in-depth studying of the printing properties of the synthesized CQDs within ink formulations. This decision was made by its exceptional optical and chemical characteristics, as previously highlighted. In the process of ink formulation, the dye loading level was adjusted to 1 wt%, while the remaining components of the ink, including solvents and cosolvents, were fine-tuned to constitute 99 wt% of the overall ink formulation. The printer employed for the investigation of these formulated inks was an Epson Stylus Photo T50. The printing is presented in Fig. 6 , shedding light on the performance and quality of the printed materials.

Before printing, the papers were thoroughly examined to ensure they exhibited no inherent fluorescence emission. The left side of the figure illustrates the appearance of the printed page under daylight conditions, while the right side showcases these printed pages under UV irradiation. The ink formulations were printed using 1, 5, 10, and 15 repetitions on the paper. Upon evaluation of the results presented in Fig. 6, it becomes evident that a single printing pass, represented by one-time printing, stands out as the optimal choice. Due to its exceptional property of not leaving any ink traces under daylight conditions, one-time printing stands out as the optimal choice for applications that require anti-counterfeiting measures. As a result, the one-time printing approach was selected for further investigations.

Water contact angle analysis was employed to study the alteration in the surface properties of paper before and after printing. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , the unprinted section of the paper exhibited a higher water contact angle (79°) compared to the printed portion (42°). The reduction in the contact angle of water on the printed area, as evidenced by the decrease from 79° to 42°, carries significant implications and can be further discussed below. Quantum dots, due to their inherent hydrophilic properties, are predisposed to interact favorably with water. When incorporated into the ink, these quantum dots facilitate strong interactions with the water component within the ink and also with the cellulose fibers on the paper's surface. The decrease in the water contact angle on the printed area suggests improved wettability. In practical terms, this means that the ink has effectively altered the paper's surface properties, making it more receptive to water.

In this research, we synthesized blue and blue-greenish N-CQDs and CQDs, utilizing citric acid as the carbon precursor and employing ethylene diamine and aniline as the doping agents through three distinct synthesis methods. These methods included a simple one-pot hydrothermal process, a microwave-assisted technique, and a simple heating synthesis method. The undoped CQD emitted light at 550 nm, while N-A-CQD exhibited a fluorescence emission peak at 480 nm, presenting a greenish-blue hue under UV illumination. Furthermore, N-E-CQD, synthesized using various routes, displayed exceptional fluorescent emissions within the 450–460 nm range. Notably, the microwave-assisted synthesis method produced the most favorable results, with an excitation range of 330 nm and emission at 460 nm. Comprehensive characterization was performed on all materials to assess their optical and chemical properties. The microwave-assisted N-E-CQD emerged as the best CQD candidate. Subsequently, we assessed the printability of this exceptional N-E-CQD using an Epson inkjet printer. The outcome of this study revealed the potential of these quantum dots as an anti-counterfeiting ink, as they exhibited outstanding fluorescence emission under UV light, serving as a robust security feature for verification. Importantly, this ink left no visible traces on papers under daylight conditions.

Faraz Mirlou Miavagh: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Amir Rezvani-Moghaddam: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources. Hossein Roghani-Mamaqani: Visualization, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Uttandaraman Sundararaj: Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
